summary: For the last thirty years, I have been a forest ecologist at the University of Kentucky. I worked primarily in research and taught forest ecology annually. I was also deeply involved in the interdisciplinary undergraduate program in natural resources and environmental sciences, even chairing it for ten years. The university has evolved to embrace sustainability more, including creating a sustainability coordinator position and supporting initiatives like the greenhouse living learning community.

entity: Department of Forestry and Natural Resources
summary: I was part of the Department of Forestry and Natural Resources at the University of Kentucky, though it was originally called the Department of Forestry when I started. My role there was mainly focused on ecological research and teaching forest ecology. The department has been central to my academic career and my efforts to promote sustainability and environmental education.
cues: Department of Forestry and Natural Resources

summary: I've lived in Lexington, Kentucky for over thirty years since accepting a faculty position at the University of Kentucky. When I first arrived, I felt Lexington was very much a sports town, but over time it has become a more interesting place with a growing culture of sustainability and progressive values. The presence of a food cooperative and university sustainability initiatives have made it a community I am proud to be part of.

I grew up in Colorado, specifically in Denver, and my early interest in environmental issues began there during high school. Later, I returned to Colorado for part of my college education at the University of Colorado and for my PhD research, which focused on subalpine spruce-fir forests in the Colorado mountains. Colorado has always felt like home and a place deeply connected to my ecological work.

Denver, Colorado, is where I grew up and developed my passion for environmental issues during my high school years. It was my home until I was eighteen, before I ventured off to college in other states. Denver remains an important part of my personal history and connection to the mountains and natural landscapes that shaped my ecological interests.

I first went to college in Maine, seeking to get far away from Colorado, but I found it too cloudy and full of trees, so I left. Later, I returned to Maine to attend Colby College, where I ultimately graduated. My time in Maine was formative, including my involvement with a food cooperative buying club and getting academic credit for teaching wilderness courses at the National Outdoor Leadership School.

After leaving Maine the first time, I spent a year off in California before returning to Colorado to continue my education. California was a brief but important transitional period in my college journey. It was part of my search for the right place and path to pursue my environmental and academic interests.

I attended the University of Colorado for a year but found it too large for my liking. However, it was significant because it allowed me to be closer to home in Denver. Later, I returned to Colorado for my PhD research affiliated with Cornell University but conducted in the Colorado mountains.

I worked at the National Outdoor Leadership School (NOLS) leading wilderness courses that lasted about thirty days. This experience was pivotal because I was teaching skills that students received academic credit for, which I later used to get credit myself at Colby College. NOLS also pioneered the Leave No Trace curriculum, which I am familiar with through connections like Drew Leemon.

I graduated from Colby College in Maine, where I convinced them to give me academic credit for my teaching work at NOLS. While at Colby, I was involved with a food cooperative buying club, which was my first exposure to cooperative food buying. Colby was an important part of my educational journey and helped shape my environmental and sustainability values.

entity: Yale School of Forestry & Environmental Studies
summary: I earned my master's degree at the Yale School of Forestry and Environmental Studies, which was an incredible place for forestry education despite some challenges within the broader university. Yale is located in New Haven, Connecticut, where I also first encountered a food cooperative that influenced my lifelong commitment to co-ops. My time at Yale was formative in deepening my ecological knowledge and professional development.

I pursued my PhD at Cornell University mainly because I found a mentor willing to let me conduct my research in Colorado. Cornell is located in Ithaca, New York, where I also experienced a strong food cooperative community that had to rebuild after a fire. My doctoral research focused on the ecosystem ecology of a subalpine spruce-fir forest in Colorado, combining my academic interests with my home state.

My PhD research focused on understanding the ecosystem ecology of a subalpine spruce-fir forest in the mountains of Colorado. This project allowed me to work in my home state while pursuing advanced ecological research. It was a key part of my academic training and deepened my expertise in forest ecology.

I had a job interview in Utah during my job search after graduate school, but it came too late in my pregnancy for me to travel there. Although I did not end up working in Utah, I have worked in mountain ranges in Utah during my time with NOLS. Utah represents part of the western mountain environments I have been connected to professionally.

I worked extensively out of the Lander, Wyoming office for NOLS, leading wilderness courses in various mountain ranges in Wyoming and Utah. Wyoming was a significant location for my early career in outdoor leadership and environmental education. It helped solidify my connection to mountain ecosystems and wilderness ethics.

I believe you might be referring to Drew Leemon, who was involved with NOLS and the pioneering of the Leave No Trace curriculum. I am familiar with the name and the connection to Leave No Trace, which is a philosophy of minimizing human impact in wilderness areas. Drew Leemon would be around my age and has been influential in outdoor ethics.

entity: Leave No Trace Center for Outdoor Ethics
summary: The Leave No Trace Center for Outdoor Ethics was pioneered by NOLS, where I worked, and it promotes traveling in the wilderness without leaving a trace. I know people connected to NOLS who have been involved with Leave No Trace, including Drew Leemon. This organization represents an important part of the outdoor education and environmental ethics framework I have been part of.

I know that a friend of mine has a father who worked for NOLS and that connection led him to work for NOLS in New Zealand. While I have not worked there myself, New Zealand is part of the broader global network connected to NOLS and outdoor leadership. It shows how the outdoor ethics and leadership principles I have been involved with extend internationally.

I lived in New Haven while attending Yale School of Forestry and Environmental Studies. It was there that I was introduced to the concept of food cooperatives, which I have been a member of ever since. New Haven was an important place for both my academic and personal development, especially in sustainability and community food systems.

Ithaca, New York, is where Cornell University is located, and I lived there while pursuing my PhD. The city had a fantastic food cooperative that had to rebuild after a fire, which deepened my understanding and commitment to cooperative food systems. Ithaca felt like a progressive place, and I initially thought it was far ahead of Lexington in embracing sustainability values.

My first exposure to food cooperatives was at Colby College, where my friends had a food co-op buying club. Later, in New Haven and Ithaca, I became a lifelong member of food co-ops, which have been central to how I buy food. When I interviewed for my job in Lexington, knowing there was a food co-op here was a key factor in deciding to move. Food co-ops have always represented a model of community and sustainability for me.

entity: College of Arts and Sciences, University of Kentucky
summary: The College of Arts and Sciences at the University of Kentucky was one of the funding bodies for the greenhouse living learning community I helped lead. Although the community lasted only a couple of years, it was an important initiative to engage students in sustainability on campus. This college's support reflected a growing institutional interest in sustainability during my tenure.

entity: College of Agriculture, Food and Environment, University of Kentucky
summary: Alongside the College of Arts and Sciences, the College of Agriculture, Food and Environment at the University of Kentucky helped fund the greenhouse living learning community. This collaboration showed a cross-college commitment to sustainability education. The college's involvement was part of broader efforts to integrate environmental and agricultural concerns on campus.
cues: College of Agriculture, Food and Environment

summary: Shane Tedder has been the sustainability coordinator at the University of Kentucky, a position that was created after much effort by many people, including those involved in the Natural Resources Program. While I am not sure how many years Shane has held the position, his role symbolizes the university's growing commitment to sustainability. This position is part of the cultural shift toward sustainability that I have witnessed during my career.
